coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived functional carbon materials for supercapacitor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Rawat, Shivam
Mishra, Rakesh K.
Bhaskar, Thallada
coredata.dc:description: Biochar produced from the thermochemical conversion of biomass, provides a green and sustainable platform for the preparation of various functional carbon materials (porous carbon, heteroatom doped biochar, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon quantum dots, etc.) towards advanced application. Their preparation involves the physical as well as chemical activation of biochar or directly from the biomass. The inherent versatile physicochemical properties of these versatile materials have been explored for the construction of the electrochemical energy storage devices like supercapacitors. In the present review, the various methodologies for the preparation of various biomass-derived carbon materials are summarized. Further utilization of these materials in supercapacitor electrodes and the properties associated with their charge storage ability, along with associated challenges and perspectives are also discussed.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived functional carbon materials for supercapacitor applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biochar produced from the thermochemical conversion of biomass, provides a green and sustainable platform for the preparation of various functional carbon materials (porous carbon, heteroatom doped biochar, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon quantum dots, etc.) towards advanced application. Their preparation involves the physical as well as chemical activation of biochar or directly from the biomass. The inherent versatile physicochemical properties of these versatile materials have been explored for the construction of the electrochemical energy storage devices like supercapacitors. In the present review, the various methodologies for the preparation of various biomass-derived carbon materials are summarized. Further utilization of these materials in supercapacitor electrodes and the properties associated with their charge storage ability, along with associated challenges and perspectives are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Global warming, environmental pollution, and the non-renewable nature of energy resources have triggered an awareness worldwide among the people to look for the alternate resources for fuels, value added chemicals, and energy. Biomass has risen as a magnificent alternate in this aspect. It refers to a plant or animal-derived organic matter, which is typically considered as waste. Approximately 181 billion tonnes of biomass waste is produced annually across the globe (Dahmen et al., 2019). Being renewable in nature, its utilization for the production of fuels, chemicals, and functional materials is highly desirable. Several strategies have been investigated for the effective utilization of these biomass, varying from biological to thermochemical conversion. The thermochemical conversion includes the steps dominated by thermal degradation into liquid, solid and gaseous products. Pyrolysis, hydrothermal conversion, and gasification are the common thermochemical strategies for converting biomass to fuels and biochemicals (Ong et al., 2019) and these processes lead to the formation of a significant amount of biochar.

Biochar is a carbon-rich solid produced as a by-product from the thermochemical conversion of biomass in an oxygen-limiting environment. The process by which the thermochemical conversion is performed depends on the type of biomass being utilized. Biomass with low moisture content is typically well suited for pyrolysis, while biomass having higher moisture content can be valorized using hydrothermal conversion methods. The physical and chemical properties of biochar vary significantly with the feedstock as well as preparation conditions. Generally, most of the biomasses can be used to produce the biochar, however, the lignocellulosic biomass with major component of cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose is a better precursor since it is devoid of starch-based edible components.

Thermochemical biorefineries produce biochar as a direct by-product during the production of biochemicals from biomass. In contrast, the biochemical biorefineries produce highly recalcitrant lignin residues resulting from microbial, enzymatic treatment that can also serve as a significant precursor for biochar production. Other sources such as algal biomass, sewage sludge, and municipal solid waste have also been utilized to produce biochar (Yu et al., 2017).

Biochar is a functionally diverse material with varying physicochemical properties leading to numerous potential applications. The use of biochar is not only limited to soil amendment (Wang et al., 2020c) but also in CO2 capture (Dissanayake et al., 2020), wastewater remediation by the removal of organic pollutants (Dai et al., 2019), and heavy metals (Zhang et al., 2020). Furthermore, it has also been utilized as a catalyst or solid support for active metals (Kumar et al., 2020a). Moreover, recent research on biochar-derived carbon materials has expanded its applications in the field of electrochemical energy storage and generation in the form of supercapacitors and fuel cells, respectively (Liu et al., 2019). Supercapacitors are electrochemical energy storage devices with a high power density and can be charged or discharged rapidly (Libich et al., 2018). Supercapacitors have many applications in electronic devices, hybrid electric vehicles, medical equipment, etc. (Banerjee et al., 2020). In addition, these can help to prevent power fluctuation problems also which is a significant issue in wind energy conversion systems (Panhwar et al., 2020). Commercially available supercapacitors suffer drawbacks related to their low energy density and high production cost (Vijayakumar et al., 2018). Therefore, the production of such devices from renewable and sustainable carbon sources is highly economical and desirable. Recent developments in supercapacitor application of biomass-derived carbon materials have demonstrated the ease of increasing the energy density of the prepared electrodes by using different electrolytic systems (Karamanova et al., 2021). Reviews focussing on the porosity of the biomass derived activated carbons have been reported recently (Yang et al., 2019a; Saini et al., 2021). However, apart from porous structure, other aspects of the charge storage mechanism in various biomass derived carbon materials needs to be addressed. Moreover, versatile forms of carbons can be produced from biomass which show varying charge storage capabilities. The focus of this review is to provide recent developments in the preparation of biomass-derived carbon materials from renewable feedstock and properties associated with its charge storage ability. A brief outline of this review is shown in Fig. 1 .

To produce biochar in an economically feasible manner, the origin and nature of feedstocks play a critical role. Depending upon the feedstock, the production techniques can be applied. Different sources for biochar production can be pointed out based on their availability and generation in a global scenario. Some of these are discussed in the following sections.

Agro-forest residue accounts for the large amount of waste generated in the form of lignocellulosic biomass. Forest residue includes the waste generated by forest-based industries after wood harvesting, such as stems, treetops, branches, etc. Agricultural residue refers to the waste generated during crop harvesting, including rice straw, wheat straw, rice husk, and corn stover. It is used as fodder for livestock or left as such in the vast agricultural land for microbial degradation. However, due to the lack of a proper disposal strategy in the developing countries, a common practice that is being followed is simply burning it and thereby causing increased CO2 levels in the atmosphere leading to adverse effects on human health and the environment (Abdurrahman et al., 2020). Thermochemical conversion by pyrolysis, gasification, hydrothermal liquefaction offers a suitable route to mitigate the adverse environmental impact of such residues by converting them into value-added products. Therefore, agro-forest residues can be considered as a sustainable feedstock for biochar production and application.

There are many industrial sectors where the wastes generated are rich in carbon content. These include forest-based industries which produce sawdust, agro-industries that generate residues such as potato peel, orange peel, oilseed cakes, etc. The pulp and paper industry generates approximately 50 million tons of lignin as a by-product annually, and less than 5% is commercially utilized, while the rest is burnt as a solid fuel (Demuner et al., 2019). Also, second-generation biorefineries, that produce bioethanol from cellulose generate lignin as a by-product. The chemical structure of the obtained lignin depends upon the source as well as the pre-treatment conditions applied to fractionate the components (Rawat et al., 2021). High value-added chemicals and materials from these carbon-rich industrial residues are highly desirable due to the large amount generated.

Sewage sludge generated during the treatment of sewage wastewater in municipal plants, can serve as a potential resource for the biochar production. It is directly related to the human population. Primary disposal methods for sewage sludge are landfill, fertilizer, and incineration. However, these methods are hazardous and limited where land availability is poor (Adhikari et al., 2018). Biochar derived from wastewater sludge is typically high in H/C ratio along with higher concentrations of nitrogen, phosphorous, and potassium compared to biochar derived from biomass (Liu et al., 2018). Also, the increase in pyrolysis temperature did not change the phosphorous, and potassium content present in the biochar (Yang et al., 2018).

The poultry litter and livestock manure are among the few types of organic waste generated from animal husbandry industry. They are rich in heteroatoms along with heavy metals like Cu and Zn, which get introduced as a part of dietary supplement of the livestock (Arauzo et al., 2020). Increasing concentration of heavy metals in soil limits the utilization of livestock manure as fertilizer to a certain extent. Thermal conversion of livestock manure to biochar helps in immobilization of these heavy metals into the biochar matrix (Li et al., 2020a). Energy storage devices in the form of supercapacitors provide a possible utilization of such biochar as the presence of transition metals can enhance the charge storage properties of the biochar (Vijayaraghavan, 2021).

Algal biomass refers to the aquatic microorganisms and aquatic plants mainly present in the ocean. It is also considered as third-generation biofuel feedstock (Chowdhury et al., 2019). Increasing studies on biological and thermochemical routes, targeting the exploitation of algal biomass for the production of fuels and chemicals have been explored, and combined processes have been suggested for its maximum utilization (Fan et al., 2020a). Algal biomass has certain advantages over land-grown biomass as a feedstock for biochar and biochemical production. It has a short harvesting cycle and does not require a vast area of land for its cultivation. The biochar obtained from algal feedstock exhibits different characteristics from lignocellulose-derived biochar. Commonly, the algal biochar is nitrogen-rich, has a higher pH with a greater H/C and O/C ratio compared to the lignocellulose-derived biochar (Yu et al., 2018a). Hence, preparing carbon materials from algal feedstock is beneficial due to inherent heteroatom functionalities. However, the type of algal biomass used should also be considered while producing biochar-based materials as its chemical composition is species-dependent.

Thermochemical methods are the most widely used techniques for the biochar production. These include pyrolysis, gasification, hydrothermal carbonization, and torrefaction. Microwave-assisted pyrolysis has also been reported for the production of biochar. An appropriate technique is chosen based on the feedstock type, for obtaining the high yield and desired quality of biochar. Process parameters such as heating rate, residence time, and temperature play a critical role in determining the physicochemical characteristics of the biochar.

It refers to the thermal decomposition of biomass under an inert atmosphere to yield biochar, bio-oil, and syngas in the temperature range of 300–900 °C. The amount of biochar produced depends upon the feedstock's nature and operating conditions (Wei et al., 2019a). Pyrolysis is further sub-divided into slow as well as fast pyrolysis based upon the temperature and heating rate. Slow pyrolysis operates at a low heating rate (5–7 °C/min) and longer residence time (>1 h), while fast pyrolysis is carried out at a greater heating rate (100–200 °C/min) with short residence time (<10 s) (Dhyani and Bhaskar, 2018). Slow pyrolysis of lignocellulosic biomass yields a higher amount of biochar, while a higher amount of bio-oil is obtained from fast pyrolysis (Al Arni, 2018). In the case of sewage sludge pyrolysis, higher biochar yield was observed at lower temperatures, while higher temperatures, on the other hand, caused an increase in the gas yield. Liquid product yield was independent of pyrolysis temperature. The biochar obtained was alkaline and had mainly macropores (Inguanzo et al., 2002).

Utilization of biomass with high moisture content or wet biomass for biochar production often involves its initial drying, an energy-intensive step. To avoid this step, hydrothermal carbonization is performed to obtain the biochar, often referred to as hydrochar. Hydrothermal biomass conversion can be controlled by varying the operating conditions to obtain solid, liquid, and gaseous products in the desired ratio. Hydrothermal carbonization is performed at a relatively lower temperature range of 180–250 °C and a pressure range of 2–10 MPa (Funke and Ziegler, 2010). Increasing the temperature to 250–350 °C and pressure to 10–30 MPa leads to a decrease in char and an increase in bio-oil yield. However, the process is then referred to as hydrothermal liquefaction (Nizamuddin et al., 2017). The physicochemical characteristics of hydrochar differ significantly from biochar, such as its structure is rich in alkyl moieties while biochar has mainly aromatic structure (Fu et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020b). Hydrochar is also rich in oxygen-containing functionalities compared to biochar obtained from the same source (Liu et al., 2010). Importantly, the functional groups on the biochar surface can also be modified by changing the solvent medium of hydrothermal carbonization. For example, the presence of sulphuric acid in the solvent system imparted sulphonate groups on the surface of biochar obtained from kraft lignin (Nunes et al., 2020).

It involves heat treatment of biomass at 250–300 °C for 10–100 min and is often referred to as mild pyrolysis (Inari et al., 2007). Torrefaction can be performed in an inert as well as in a controlled oxidative environment. In the presence of oxygen, yield for oil and gas was increased instead of biochar when compared to torrefaction in a nitrogen environment (Chen et al., 2020). In general, torrefaction is performed to improve the energy density of the biochar, i.e., to increase its high heating value (Sukiran et al., 2020). Thus, the torrefied solid obtained is energy-dense and is known as “green coal” or “bio-coal” and preferentially co-fired in boilers along with coal (Acharya et al., 2015; Barskov et al., 2019).

The conventional process of biochar preparation, such as pyrolysis, requires high temperatures and often it is a lengthy process. Compared to it, flash carbonization is performed at a typically lower temperature of up to 400 °C, and once the desired temperature is achieved, the biochar is exposed to air for a short residence time of 10–20 min (Antal et al., 2003; Hirst et al., 2018). In this process, the biomass is placed in a packed bed at elevated pressure. Then, the biomass is further ignited from below against the airflow direction (Nunoura et al., 2006). It is an efficient process for the conversion of biomass into biochar.

This process involves the thermochemical conversion of biomass to syngas (CO, CO2, CH4, and H2) production at elevated temperatures in the presence of steam or air. It offers less biochar yield, and a high yield of syngas (Prabakar et al., 2018). The biochar obtained from steam gasification of lignocellulose has a highly microporous structure and is rich in mineral content due to partial oxidation of the feedstock (Romero Millán et al., 2019). The properties of biochar obtained from gasification can also be tuned by optimizing the operating conditions directing the sustainable production of both syngas and biochar (You et al., 2017).

It is an alternative to the conventional heating methods. Biomass with high moisture content (aquatic biomass, sewage sludge, etc.) can be utilized to produce the biochar, bio-oil, and syngas using microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Instantaneous heating of feedstock can be achieved through this method which offers an advantage over other thermochemical methods (Ethaib et al., 2020). A comparative study of microwave-assisted pyrolysis and slow pyrolysis on the biochar properties was performed by Zhu et al. using corn stover as feedstock. The biochar produced by the former method showed a higher surface area compared to slow pyrolysis along with a higher amount of surface carbonyl groups (Zhu et al., 2015). Microwave-assisted pyrolysis of sewage sludge showed fewer micro-mesopores than conventional pyrolysis (Menéndez et al., 2005). While in the case of oil palm waste, microwave-assisted pyrolysis at 700 W generated the mesoporous biochar with 210 m2/g surface area (Liew et al., 2018).

The process parameters such as temperature, heating rate, and residence time can significantly modify biochar surface properties such as functional groups, surface area, or porosity. Infrared studies and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis of various biochar showed the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and aromatic moieties (Figueredo et al., 2017). Generally, increasing the pyrolytic temperatures results in decreasing the oxygen content. Moreover, the surface area obtained is greater at higher temperatures (Mendonça et al., 2017). The pH of biochar can also be manipulated by changing the process parameters, as demonstrated by Zhao et al. It was reported in the study that biochar produced from apple tree branches at different temperatures resulted in the increase in the pH at higher temperature due to a decrease in carboxyl groups at elevated temperatures (Zhao et al., 2017b). In general, the pH depends on the acidic and basic functionalities developed on the surface of biochar during thermal methods. Furthermore, the elemental composition of biochar is not only limited to C, H, and O. Heteroatoms such as S, N, P, etc., are also present in biochar depending upon the feedstock and plays a significant impact on the properties of biochar, such as its pH, resulting in varying charge storage tendencies. The electrical conductivity of biochar is directly linked to graphitization content. Increasing the pyrolysis temperature to 1000 °C produced the graphitized biochar with high electrical conductivity (Gabhi et al., 2020).

Carbon-based electrode materials are pretty common due to their affordable economy and availability. Moreover, the ease of modification and manipulation of pore structure, surface functionality, and surface area provides another advantage for applying carbon materials in energy storage devices. In supercapacitors, the mechanism of charge storage is the electrostatic attraction of electrolyte ions onto the surface of electrode material and therefore, optimum pores compatible with the size of the corresponding ions are the prerequisite (Young et al., 2018). The surface functionalities such as carboxylic, hydroxy, or N and P generated functional groups provide another aspect of charge storage through electrochemical redox reactions of these functional groups termed pseudocapacitance. Moreover, the degree of graphitization is also an essential parameter for the supercapacitor application of carbon-based material since increased graphitization results in high electrical conductivity (Xing et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019b). The structural stability of the carbon framework provides long-lasting cyclic stability and capacitance retention of the electrode material (Šedajová et al., 2020).

The biochar produced from thermochemical processes as mentioned above, often requires physical or chemical activation to enhance its surface functionalities or other features such as specific surface area or porosity which ultimately improve its charge storage applications. Physical activation involves the treatment of biochar with ammonia, steam, CO2, or a limited amount of air at temperatures between 600 and 900 °C. These activating streams of gases react with the carbon atoms present on the surface of biochar. As the reaction proceeds between the carbon and the activation agent, micropores and mesopores are generated (Sajjadi et al., 2019). Chemical activation involves the treatment of biochar with acid, bases, or metal salts, etc., at a temperature range of 450–900 °C. Commonly used activating agents include, NaOH, KOH, ZnCl2, and H3PO4. The chemical activation produces biochar with new surface functionalities and well-defined pore size distribution, which can be controlled depending upon the activating agent and process condition. Also, the ratio of biochar to the activating agent plays a significant role in the porosity development (Angın et al., 2013). In this section, different biochar-derived material, their types, and electrochemical charge storage capacities have been discussed in detail and summarized in Tables 1 and 2
.

Direct use of biochar for charge storage processes is not worthwhile due to its low surface area and lack of appropriate pore size distribution. Due to this, activation is performed to generate the pores on the biochar material. Based on their pore size, different types of porous materials derived from biomass have been discussed below.

The microporous carbon materials with pore size below 2 nm were prepared from almond nutshells derived biochar by KOH and HNO3 activation. The specific surface area of KOH and HNO3 activated biochar was 1363 and 327.7 m2/g, respectively exhibiting their respective specific capacitance as 272.3 and 286.1 F/g at 1 A/g (Wu et al., 2016). Carbonization of flaxseed residue at 600 °C, followed by the KOH activation at 700 °C, produced the microporous carbon material with a surface area of 3230 m2/g. The obtained material achieved a specific capacitance of 369 F/g at 1 A/g (Li et al., 2020e). Ultra-microporous materials with a pore size of less than 0.7 nm (Grau-Marin et al., 2020) have found multiple applications in the field of gas-phase separation/adsorption. Production of such porous materials from biomass precursors has been reported in a recent study where the KOH treated bacterial cellulose was activated using thiourea to produce ultra-microporous carbon material. The prepared material showed high specific and volumetric capacitance of 430 F/g and 507 F/cm3, respectively (Ding et al., 2020).

Another mesoporous biochar with a surface area of 630 m2/g was prepared using crab shells as the precursor for carbon. Here, the inherent CaCO3 present in the crab shell acted as an in-situ template for the mesopore generation through decomposition to CaO and CO2. The specific capacitance of 220 F/g was achieved at the pyrolysis temperature of 900 °C (Gao et al., 2018). Carbonization of silkworm excrements at 700 °C and pickling with HCl produced the mesoporous biochar with a specific capacitance of 391.5 F/g (Zhang et al., 2019b).

Hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) are materials with interconnected pores over a wide pore size distribution ranging from micro, meso, and macropores. This well-connected pore arrangement provides a suitable application in the field of diffusion-controlled systems, such as energy storage in electric double layer supercapacitors where the charge storage mechanism depends on electrostatic adsorption-desorption of ions on the surface of such materials (Inagaki et al., 2014). Waste biomass activation using templates can provide HPC materials for such applications (Xiaoli et al., 2020). One-pot synthesis of such HPC was achieved by biomass treatment with KHCO3 and carbonization at 900 °C, and a specific capacitance of 253 F/g was achieved (Deng et al., 2015). A template-free method for preparation of HPC from corn husk was reported by Song et al. where the husk was refluxed in KOH, and the cellulose-rich residue thus obtained was directly carbonized to obtain the carbon material. The specific capacitance was 356 F/g at 1 A/g current density (Song et al., 2015). Steam exploded wheat straw upon carbonization produced HPC foam at 400 °C, and activation by KOH resulted in HPC with 226.2 F/g specific capacitance at 0.5 A/g (Gou et al., 2020). Further, lignosulfonate was utilized to prepare hierarchical porous carbon materials using mesoporous silica as a template and ZnCl2 as an activating agent. The prepared carbon showed a specific capacitance of 298 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g (Bai et al., 2020). The HPC composite prepared from K2FeO4 activated lignosulfonate with alizarin red derivative showed the redox facilitated specific capacitance of 469.5 F/g at 0.5 A/g (Wang et al., 2021).

Doping of biochar with p-block elements such as N, P, or S using organic or inorganic precursors has also been performed to enhance its electrochemically active sites and increase the wettability of electrode surface with electrolytes (Nirosha et al., 2020). As mentioned above, the presence of these heteroatom can also be inherent to the biomass, leading to the biochar with different elemental compositions based on the process parameters and feedstock. Moreover, the functionalities generated by the heteroatoms can also be modified with the preparation conditions. For instance, the nitrogen in the biochar can be transformed from pyrrolic to pyridinic and quaternary nitrogen through polymerization and condensation at high temperatures (Yu et al., 2018b). Nitrogen-rich biochar was prepared from lignosulfonate-phenylenediamine mixture pyrolysis at 400 °C for 2 h. The prepared biochar exhibited 480 F/g specific capacitance at 1A/g current density and volumetric capacitance of 950 F/cm3 (Zhang et al., 2019a). Non-thermal plasma modification of lilac and lotus seedpods in nitrogen atmosphere was reported to increase the nitrogen content in the obtained biochar by 10 and 3.6 times with the specific capacitance of the 214.5 and 201.1 F/g respectively (Wu et al., 2021). Bean pulp in-situ activation with CO2 at 500 °C for 30 min produced the biochar with rich nitrogen content. The CO2 activation resulted in the well-developed hierarchical porosity through opening and expansion of pores. At the same time, the nitrogen content in biochar also doubled when CO2 volume was increased. The presence of pyridine and pyrrole moieties in the biochar enhanced the pseudocapacitance and a specific capacitance of 105 F/g was achieved at 0.25 A/g current density (Ding et al., 2021). Nitrogen doping of biochar from lecithin by hydrothermal carbonization and KOH activation was performed using urea as the nitrogen precursor, resulting into increase in the specific capacitance by almost 16% for the nitrogen-doped biochar (Demir et al., 2017). Hydrochar prepared from waste biomass was also activated by the molten salt process. The presence of inherent nitrogen and the specific capacitance of 447 F/g at 0.2 A/g were observed in the biochar (Lei et al., 2021). Biochar prepared from mantis shrimp shell by pyrolysis at 750 °C was rich in nitrogen and sulphur with a specific capacitance of 201 F/g at 1 A/g (Huang et al., 2021). Poultry litter-derived biochar upon activation by KOH in the inert atmosphere produced the carbon material having heteroatoms such as P, N, and S, displayed a specific capacitance of 229 F/g (Pontiroli et al., 2019). Pyrolysis of biomass impregnated with NH4H2PO4 resulted in N and P doped biochar with a specific capacitance of 181.3 F/g and 1 A/g (Li et al., 2020b).

Transition metal oxides have been reported to show great potential in the field of energy storage devices. The variable oxidation states of metals help in charge storage through electrochemical reactions, while in the case of carbon, the charge storage mechanism is predominantly electrostatic. Therefore, the combined effect can be achieved in metal-doped biochar. The redox reactions due to the metal incorporated biochar enhance the specific capacitance while the carbon support provides a high surface area for the electroactive material. Biochar containing α-Fe3O4 was prepared using FeCl3 and pyrolysis at 800 °C for 2 h. The prepared biochar was able to store a charge of 452.3 F/g at 2 A/g current density. The specific capacitance of the loaded biochar was higher than that of α-Fe3O4 (Li et al., 2020c). Biochar prepared from banana stems at 500 °C was converted to magnetic biochar using FeCl3 and FeSO4. The surface of biochar was modified with magnetite nanoparticles, which resulted in a specific capacitance of 333 F/g (Thomas et al., 2020). Activation of algal biochar with FeCl3 and KOH resulted in biochar loaded with iron nanoparticles and an interconnected porous network which shows the specific capacitance of 418 F/g at 1 A/g (Pourhosseini et al., 2018). Supercapacitor electrodes prepared from algal biochar followed by NaOH activation and impregnation with cobalt displayed a specific capacitance of 445 F/g at 1 A/g (Norouzi et al., 2021). Ni-loaded biochar prepared from sewage sludge and dairy manure by pyrolysis at 600 °C was further activated by microwave oxidation, that led the increase in the specific capacitance up to two times (Wang et al., 2017b). This suggests the great potential of metal-doped biochar in the fabrication of electrode materials for supercapacitors.

CNT refers to the class of carbon materials assembled in cylindrical tubes with diameters varying from 0.8 to 2 nm for single-walled CNT while 5–20 nm for multi-walled CNT (Lu and Liu, 2006; Nagai et al., 2011). CNTs have found various applications in the field of biosensors, drug delivery applications, and biomedical imaging (Merum et al., 2017). CNTs have also been investigated for supercapacitor electrode preparations. The one-dimensional structure of CNTs offers long-range pathways for the effective transport of the electrolytes (Li et al., 2018). CNTs also increase the ionic conductivity and reduce the self-discharge of the supercapacitor electrode, which is responsible for the potential drop and energy loss (Fan et al., 2020b). The most common method for synthesizing these carbon materials is chemical vapour deposition, but the carbon sources used are non-renewable hydrocarbons such as methane, ethane, acetylene, xylene, etc. Large-scale production is still a significant concern, along with the choice of precursors involved. Biomass-derived CNT-based supercapacitor electrodes have also been prepared and shown excellent specific capacitance (Table 2). CNTs can also be prepared by the biochar activation as reported by Osman et al. where it was prepared from activated biochar from potato peel waste and Miscanthus biomass using iron oxalate and melamine by further heat treatment at 900 °C (Osman et al., 2019, 2020). Biochar obtained from agro-industrial residue at 600 °C was converted to CNTs using ferrocene as catalyst under microwave irradiation at 80 °C (Hidalgo et al., 2019). The CNTs have also been prepared from biomass directly using microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Multi-walled CNTs were prepared using microwave-assisted pyrolysis of gumwood using silicon carbide at 500 °C, while in the absence of microwave irradiation, no such structures were formed (Shi et al., 2014). Cobalt and nitrogen-doped CNTs were prepared from chitosan by carbonization at 800 °C under argon flow. CH4 and CO were investigated for the production of CNT structures using Ni–Mg/Al2O3 catalyst. Both CO and CH4 provided synergistic effects in producing CNTs at 550 °C in a fluidized bed reactor suggesting the feasibility of synthesizing CNTs from biomass gasification products (Zhang et al., 2018).

Graphene refers to the single-layered graphitic sheet of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. It has a large surface area, high conductivity, and thermal stability, making it suitable for the supercapacitor application. Under optimal distance between graphene nanosheets, high diffusion of electrolyte ions results in the high specific capacitance (Yu et al., 2019). The graphene's current preparation techniques are based on its extraction from graphite using mechanical exfoliation, liquid-phase exfoliation, and oxidation-reduction methods (Ren et al., 2018). Other methods include chemical vapour deposition of graphene over transition metals such as copper and nickel. However, the extraction of the graphene layer from the metal surface and large-scale production remains a major issue (Chen et al., 2017). The utilization of different renewable precursors like industrial and agricultural wastes in the preparation of graphene has also been reported. Solid carbon sources such as food waste, animal waste, etc., have been successfully utilized to prepare graphene over copper foil (Ruan et al., 2011). KOH-activated biochar from waste peanut shells was converted to few-layered graphene by mechanical exfoliation and subsequent sonication with H2SO4. The material obtained was applied for supercapacitor electrode with a specific capacitance of 78.3 F/g at 1 A/g current density and energy density of 68 W h /Kg (Purkait et al., 2017). Utilization of KOH for activation and catalyst for the graphitization of biochar produced the porous graphene sheets with a specific capacitance of 240 F/g at 1 A/g (Nanaji et al., 2021). Vertically aligned graphene nanosheets were obtained from spruce bark by hydrothermal carbonization and subsequent KOH activation lead to a high specific capacitance of 398 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g (Sun et al., 2018). Graphene-based carbon prepared by glucose carbonization with NH4Cl at 400 °C and further activation by KOH and CO2 displayed a specific surface area of 3657 m2/g and specific capacitance of 175 F/g (Jung et al., 2018). Introducing oxygen-containing functional groups can further enhance the specific capacitance of graphene-based electrodes (Cao et al., 2018). The studies have shown facile synthetic procedure for the preparation of these exquisite carbon materials with environmentally benign methodologies and demand further development in large-scale preparations.

CQDs are carbon nanoparticles with fluorescent properties and belong to a relatively new class of carbon materials that have found widespread applications in chemical sensors, biosensors, bioimaging, nanomedicines, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis (Lim et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019). Interestingly, CQDs and their composites have also attracted interest in the field of supercapacitor applications (Permatasari et al., 2021). Having high acidic functional groups such as –COOH and –OH, these materials increase the hydrophilicity of the electrode materials. CQDs have also been reportedly used as electrolytes in supercapacitors (Zhang et al., 2016). Biochar derived from different feedstocks after post-treatment using oxidizing agents such as KMnO4 lead to CQDs formation (Meng et al., 2019). CQDs produced from banana peels via a one-step hydrothermal process, has been used in the preparation of supercapacitor electrodes with the specific capacitance of 199 F/g at 0.5 A/g (Nguyen et al., 2020). Recently, extraction of CQD from empty fruit bunch-derived biochar was reported by simple hydrothermal extraction in isopropyl alcohol at 250 °C for 1 h (Jamaludin et al., 2020). With the help of microwaves, the extraction of CQDs from biochar was achieved in a few minutes (Zaman et al., 2021). Visible light-induced increase in the specific capacitance of carbon dots derived supercapacitors was also reported recently. A specific capacitance of 312 F/g at 0.1 A/g was achieved in the presence of visible light, which was more than double that obtained in the absence of light (Wang et al., 2020a). Cauliflower-derived CQD composite with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) showed a specific capacitance of 278 F/g at 0.2 A/g (Hoang et al., 2019). The incorporation of CQDs with Nickel sulphide also enhanced the specific capacitance of the supercapacitor electrode to 800 F/g (Sahoo et al., 2018).

Conversion of biomass to biochar followed by the activation via various physical and chemical processes yields diverse functional materials with different functionalities with varying charge storage capacities. Among the numerous sources of raw materials available, the quality and consistency of the produced biochar, are a major concern and thus to ensure the suitability of biochar for a specific application, a constant homogeneity of the raw material is highly desired. For instance, in the case of sewage sludge, the organic matter content is highly variable. Therefore, the choice of feedstock, the process for activation at industrial scale, needs to be explored extensively and requires further investigation as mentioned below.
•
The inorganics along with other impurities present in the biomass that can accumulate during the preparation of biochar-based materials also need to be looked carefully as their presence can significantly alter the specific capacitance (Duignan and Zhao, 2019).
•
The combined effect of biochar-based carbon materials in their composite electrode forms with various electrolytes also needs to be investigated as the interaction of different organic, ionic liquids, and quasi-solid-state electrolytes have great impact on the charge storage efficiency.
•
For the materials, exhibiting both the electrostatic and electrochemical charge storage behaviour (e.g., heteroatom-doped biochar or metal-doped biochar) the precise control over phase, surface functional groups, and the morphology remains a major challenge to be addressed.
•
The production of exquisite materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, etc., are limited to lab-scale production only. However, the streamlined production of such material with high yield and economic feasibility is critical.

Biochar can be used as a sustainable source for producing a wide range of functional carbon materials with interesting properties, responsible for different charge storage mechanisms. The type of carbon materials prepared depends on biomass's thermochemical conversion to biochar and its subsequent activation by chemical or physical methods. These materials have successfully demonstrated their application for the preparation of supercapacitor electrodes. The diversity of carbon materials and the resulting supercapacitor properties reflected on the prepared electrode materials highlight the need to tune the process parameters to maximize their efficiency. However, much of technological advancement remains to produce such material on a large scale in a green and environmentally facile manner.
Shivam Rawat: Writing – original draft. Rakesh K. Mishra: Writing – review & editing. Thallada Bhaskar: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.
